subject: dermatology
title: Dermatology High-Yield Notes
description: High-yield dermatology revision notes across 20 topics.

sections.title: Skin structure & basic lesions
sections.html:
The skin is the largest organ (1.5–2 m², 5–10 kg): a mechanical, immunological, biochemical and microbial barrier.

Layers

  • Epidermis (from surface down): stratum corneum → granulosum → spinosum → basale. Mostly keratinocytes (≈36:1 vs melanocytes). Water falls from 70–75% (basal) to 10–15% (corneum).
  • Key cells: melanocytes (basal layer, dendritic, make melanosomes that shield keratinocyte DNA — an "umbrella"); Langerhans cells (bone-marrow-derived antigen-presenting/immune surveillance); Merkel cells (fine touch).
  • Dermo-epidermal junction: basement membrane held by hemidesmosomes (BP180/collagen XVII, BP230, α6β4 integrin, laminin-332) — the targets in sub-epidermal blistering disease.
  • Dermis: collagen + elastin, vessels, nerves, appendages (papillary + reticular).
  • Subcutis (fat): caloric reserve, insulation, cushioning.

Basic (elementary) lesions — know these cold

  • Macule – flat discoloured area. Papule – solid elevation <0.5 cm. Nodule – solid elevation >0.5 cm. Plaque – flat-topped elevation.
  • Vesicle – fluid <0.5 cm. Bulla – fluid >0.5 cm. Pustule – pus. Wheal – transient oedematous elevation (urticaria).
  • Erosion – loss of epidermis (heals without scar). Ulcer – deeper, into dermis (may scar). Crust – dried exudate. Scale – visible corneocyte flakes. Excoriation – scratch erosion. Lichenification – thickened skin with accentuated markings.

Diagnostic tools

  • Wood's light (UV 365 nm): erythrasma → coral-red; some tinea capitis → fluorescence; tinea/pityriasis versicolor; vitiligo accentuation.
  • Dermoscopy: magnification + illumination — pigment network, vessels, blue-white veil.
  • DIF/IIF, biopsy, patch testing, KOH/culture, Tzanck smear.

sections.title: Skin pathophysiology & sensation
sections.html:
Sensory receptors (from the pathophysiology lecture)

StructureLocationFunction
Merkel cells (SA-I)Basal epidermis (fingertips, lips)Fine touch, edges, texture
Meissner corpusclesDermal papillae (glabrous skin)Light touch, flutter, low-freq vibration
Pacinian corpusclesDeep dermis/subcutisDeep pressure, high-freq vibration
Ruffini endingsDermisSkin stretch, sustained pressure
Free nerve endingsEpidermis/dermisPain & temperature
  • Thermoreceptors: cold (Aδ), warm (C fibres). TRP channels: TRPV1 = heat/capsaicin; TRPM8 = cold/menthol.
  • Nociceptors: Aδ = fast sharp pain; C = slow dull pain. Mediators: substance P, CGRP, prostaglandins, bradykinin.
  • Itch (pruriception): distinct modality; mediators = histamine, IL-31, proteases, neurogenic inflammation — central in atopic dermatitis and chronic pruritus.
  • Pathways: dorsal column–medial lemniscus (fine touch, vibration, proprioception); spinothalamic tract (pain, temperature, crude touch).

Functions of skin

Barrier, sensory, immune surveillance, metabolic (vitamin D), thermoregulation (sweat, vasomotor), limits evaporation. Sebum (squalene, wax esters, cholesterol, triglycerides) lubricates and protects.

sections.title: Psoriasis
sections.html:
Chronic, relapsing, immune-mediated inflammatory disease of skin & nails with systemic comorbidities. Prevalence ~2–3%; two onset peaks (15–35 = type 1; 50–60 = type 2). Strong genetic link (PSORS1, HLA-Cw6, IL-23/IL-17 axis).

Clinical signs

  • Well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery-white scale, symmetrical, on elbows, knees, scalp, sacrum, nails.
  • Auspitz sign – punctate bleeding on scraping scale. Koebner phenomenon – lesions at sites of trauma. Pruritus/pain common.

Clinical forms

  • Plaque (vulgaris) – commonest (~80–90%).
  • Guttate – small drop-like lesions, often post-streptococcal, in children/young adults; ~25% → chronic plaque.
  • Inverse (flexural) – smooth red patches in folds, minimal scale.
  • Pustular – sterile pustules (generalized form can be life-threatening).
  • Erythrodermic – widespread; medical emergency.
  • Special sites: scalp, nails, palmoplantar, genital.

Severity & comorbidities

  • PASI and BSA (1 palm ≈ 1% BSA): mild <3%, moderate 3–10%, severe >10%. Moderate–severe = PASI ≥10 or BSA ≥10 AND DLQI ≥10.
  • Psoriatic arthritis (nail disease present in ~85–95% of PsA), metabolic syndrome, CVD, obesity, depression, IBD, uveitis.

Treatment ladder

  • Mild: topical steroids, vitamin-D analogues, calcineurin inhibitors, keratolytics.
  • Moderate–severe: phototherapy (nbUVB, PUVA); classic systemics = acitretin, cyclosporine, methotrexate; small molecules (apremilast/PDE4, deucravacitinib/TYK2).
  • Biologics: anti-TNF-α (etanercept, infliximab, adalimumab); anti-IL-12/23 p40 (ustekinumab); anti-IL-17A (secukinumab, ixekizumab, brodalumab, bimekizumab); anti-IL-23p19 (guselkumab, risankizumab, tildrakizumab). Goal = PASI 90/100.

sections.title: Eczema / dermatitis (atopic, contact, seborrheic, nummular, hand)
sections.html:
Atopic dermatitis (AD)

  • Chronic, itchy inflammatory disease; part of the atopic march (AD → asthma → rhinitis).
  • Pathogenesis: skin-barrier defect (filaggrin mutations, abnormal lipids), microbiome dysbiosis (↑S. aureus), type-2 inflammation (IL-4, IL-13, IL-31, TSLP), itch-scratch cycle.
  • Hanifin–Rajka: pruritus is the essential (major) criterion + ≥3 major/minor features.
  • Age distribution: infants → face/extensors/cheeks; children → flexures (antecubital/popliteal); adults → flexures, hands, neck, lichenification.
  • Treatment: emollients, topical steroids, topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus); phototherapy; systemic — cyclosporine, MTX, azathioprine; biologic dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα); JAK inhibitors. Eczematous children can/should be vaccinated (during remission).

Contact dermatitis

  • Irritant (ICD) – non-immune direct cellular injury; commonest occupational eczema (hairdressers, healthcare, wet work). Acute, delayed-acute, chronic-cumulative (barrier lipid depletion). Rx: avoidance, emollients, barrier repair, low-potency steroids.
  • Allergic (ACD) – Type IV delayed hypersensitivity. Sensitisation (hapten → Langerhans cell → T cells, ~3–4 wks) then elicitation (re-exposure, 24–72 h). Dx by patch test (read at 48 h & 96 h; allergic = infiltration persisting at day 7; irritant resolves). Baboon syndrome = systemic contact dermatitis of buttocks/inner thighs.

Other eczemas

  • Seborrheic dermatitis – greasy yellow scale on scalp/nasolabial folds/eyebrows; Malassezia-associated; ↑ in HIV, Parkinson's, HCV. Infantile = "cradle cap". Rx: antifungal shampoo (selenium sulfide/ketoconazole), mild steroids.
  • Nummular – coin-shaped pruritic plaques on extremities; DDx tinea corporis.
  • Hand eczema / dyshidrotic (pompholyx) – palmar vesicles, nickel-associated; ~12% prevalence.

sections.title: Lichen planus & papulosquamous
sections.html:
Lichen planus (LP)

  • The 5 Ps: Purple, Polygonal, Pruritic, Planar Papules — with Wickham striae (white lacy lines) and the Koebner phenomenon.
  • Sites: flexor wrists/forearms, ankles, shins, lower back, genitalia, oral mucosa (lacy white streaks / erosive), scalp (lichen planopilaris → scarring alopecia), nails (pterygium, ridging).
  • Associations: hepatitis C, primary biliary cirrhosis; mucosal LP has a small SCC risk. Rx: topical/systemic steroids, calcineurin inhibitors.

Pityriasis rosea

  • Self-limiting; "herald patch" then a "Christmas-tree" distribution of scaly oval patches on the trunk. Symptomatic Rx: topical steroids, antihistamines, UVB — not systemic retinoids.

Urticaria / angioedema

  • Urticaria = pruritic wheals that individually last <24 h; Rx = antihistamines (first-line).
  • Angioedema = deeper swelling; often needs systemic steroids. Hereditary angioedema = C1-inhibitor deficiency.

sections.title: Acne & rosacea
sections.html:
Acne vulgaris

  • Chronic inflammation of the pilosebaceous unit; ~80% of <20 y. Four pathogenic pillars: ↑ androgen-driven sebum, abnormal keratinocyte plugging, Cutibacterium (Propionibacterium) acnes, inflammation.
  • Comedones are the primary lesion (open = blackhead; closed = whitehead) → papules, pustules, nodules, cysts, scars (ice-pick, atrophic, keloidal).
  • Severe variants: nodulocystic, acne conglobata, acne fulminans (systemic — fever, leukocytosis, arthralgia, lytic bone lesions → may need hospitalisation). Drug-induced: corticosteroids, testosterone, cyclosporine, lithium, antiepileptics.
  • Treatment: mild = topical (benzoyl peroxide, retinoids/adapalene/tretinoin, azelaic acid, topical antibiotics); moderate = + oral antibiotics (tetracyclines, macrolides) ± hormonal (OCP, spironolactone); severe = oral isotretinoin. Cyclosporine is NOT an acne drug.

Rosacea

  • Chronic facial disease, adults 30–60; multifactorial (Demodex, H. pylori, vascular). No comedones (key differentiator from acne).
  • Features: transient flushing → persistent central erythema, telangiectasia, papulopustules, phymatous change (rhinophyma, mostly men). Ocular rosacea in >50% (dryness, blepharitis, keratitis).
  • Triggers: sun, heat/cold, stress, alcohol, spicy food. Rx: trigger avoidance, sun protection; topical metronidazole/ivermectin/azelaic acid; oral doxycycline/isotretinoin; laser (PDL, Nd:YAG).

sections.title: Autoimmune bullous diseases
sections.html:
Split by blister level. Pemphigus = intra-epidermal (acantholysis, flaccid, Nikolsky +). Pemphigoid group = sub-epidermal (tense, Nikolsky −). Diagnosis triad = clinical + histology + direct immunofluorescence (± IIF/ELISA).

Pemphigus

  • Pemphigus vulgaris (PV): anti-DSG3 (± DSG1); suprabasal split; always oral mucosa, ~50% skin; flaccid blisters/erosions; Nikolsky positive. DIF = intercellular ('chicken-wire') IgG/C3.
  • Pemphigus foliaceus: anti-DSG1; superficial (subcorneal/granular) split; no mucosal disease. Endemic form = "fogo selvagem" (Brazil).
  • Others: paraneoplastic, IgA, drug-induced (penicillamine, ACE inhibitors).

Bullous pemphigoid (BP)

  • Commonest AIBD; elderly (risk rises sharply after 60). Anti-BP180 (collagen XVII, NC16A domain) + BP230; IgG (± IgE). Very pruritic, tense blisters, Nikolsky negative, mucosa usually spared.
  • DIF = linear IgG/C3 along basement membrane. Salt-split skin IIF: IgG on epidermal (roof) side (vs EBA = dermal/floor side). Associations: neurodegenerative disease, psoriasis. Drug triggers: gliptins (DPP-4), checkpoint inhibitors, diuretics, ACE inhibitors, terbinafine.
  • Variants: mucous-membrane (cicatricial) pemphigoid (laminin-332; ~25% develop cancer), pemphigoid gestationis (pregnancy; anti-BP180; ↑ Graves risk), EBA (anti-collagen VII, dermal side).

Dermatitis herpetiformis (DH)

  • Cutaneous analogue of celiac disease; intensely pruritic grouped vesicles/excoriations on extensors (elbows, knees, buttocks).
  • Histology: sub-epidermal blister with neutrophil microabscesses in dermal papillae. DIF: granular IgA at dermal papillary tips. Antibodies: anti-tTG (TG2), anti-endomysial, anti-epidermal transglutaminase (TG3), anti-gliadin. Rx: gluten-free diet + dapsone.

sections.title: Connective tissue diseases (lupus, scleroderma, dermatomyositis)
sections.html:
Cutaneous lupus erythematosus (CLE)

  • Subtypes: acute (ACLE — malar butterfly rash, spares nasolabial folds), subacute (SCLE — drug-associated, photosensitive), chronic (CCLE — discoid LE scarring plaques). Exclude SLE before diagnosing CLE.
  • Labs: ANA, anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm, complement (C3/C4), CBC, urinalysis. Rx: strict photoprotection, stop smoking; topical steroids/calcineurin inhibitors; first-line systemic = antimalarials (hydroxychloroquine) — check G6PD, monitor retina.

Scleroderma

  • Localized (morphea): skin only, no internal organs; plaque/linear/generalized/deep. F:M ≈ 2:1. Rx topical/phototherapy/systemic immunosuppression.
  • Systemic sclerosis: diffuse vs limited cutaneous. CREST = Calcinosis, Raynaud phenomenon, Esophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, Telangiectasia.
  • Skin signs: Raynaud (usually first), sclerodactyly, digital ulcers/calcinosis, salt-and-pepper pigmentation, perioral furrowing, microstomia, beaked nose, abnormal nailfold capillaries.
  • Antibodies: ANA; anti-centromere (limited/CREST), anti-Scl-70 (topoisomerase, diffuse), anti-RNA polymerase III.

Dermatomyositis (DM)

  • Bimodal (children 5–15; adults 40–60; F>M). Skin + proximal muscle weakness.
  • Skin: Gottron's papules (over knuckles), heliotrope rash (violaceous periorbital ± oedema), shawl sign, V-sign, holster sign, periungual telangiectasia, photosensitivity.
  • Labs: ↑CK, ↑ESR/CRP, ANA, myositis antibodies (anti-Jo-1, anti-Mi-2); EMG, muscle/skin biopsy. Adult DM → screen for internal malignancy. Rx: systemic steroids, MTX/AZA, IVIG, rituximab, photoprotection.

sections.title: Skin tumours — benign, premalignant, malignant
sections.html:
Benign

  • Seborrheic keratosis – benign keratinocyte tumour; "stuck-on", warty, brain-like; older adults. Dermoscopy: milia-like cysts, comedo-like openings, fissures/ridges, fingerprint & "fat-finger" signs, moth-eaten border. Sudden eruptive SKs = sign of Leser-Trélat → internal (often GI) malignancy.
  • Solar lentigo – flat brown macules on photo-exposed sites. Acrochordon (skin tag). Angioma (red papule, vascular). Dermatofibroma – firm nodule, dimple sign. Pyogenic granuloma – rapidly growing friable bleeding red papule = benign lobular capillary hemangioma (trauma/pregnancy).

Premalignant / in-situ

  • Actinic (solar) keratosis – red scaly macules on sun-exposed skin, fair skin; SCC precursor; does NOT itself metastasize. Bowen disease = SCC in situ.

Basal cell carcinoma (BCC)

  • Most common skin cancer; locally invasive, rarely metastasizes. Nodular (pearly nodule, telangiectasia, rolled border, "rodent ulcer", head/neck) or superficial (trunk). ~50% pigmented.
  • Dermoscopy: arborising vessels, blue-grey ovoid nests/globules, leaf-like areas, spoke-wheel, no pigment network. Originates from epidermal basal layer/follicular epithelium.

Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC)

  • Strongest association with cumulative sun exposure. Keratotic/ulcerated nodule. Metastatic potential: skin ~0.5%, mucosa ~16%, on scars/burns/chronic infection ~18% → first to regional lymph nodes.
  • Risks: chronic UV, immunosuppression (transplant), scars/chronic wounds (Marjolin), HPV, radiation, arsenic. Not metabolic syndrome. Rx: excision to clear margins (Mohs high-risk).

sections.title: Nevi & melanoma
sections.html:
Nevi

  • Benign melanocyte proliferations. Acquired mature: junctional (flat) → compound → dermal (nodular) — three phases; dermal is the end-stage.
  • Special: dysplastic/atypical (asymmetric, multi-coloured, irregular border; any age; sporadic or FAMMM — melanoma marker); blue nevus (Tyndall effect); Spitz/Reed (pink-red raised, children); Sutton (halo) nevus (depigmented rim, immune-mediated, rarely transforms).
  • Congenital nevi (~1%): giant >20 cm carry the highest melanoma-within-nevus risk.

Melanoma

  • Malignant neural-crest melanocyte tumour; de novo (most) or from a nevus. ~1500 new cases/yr in Greece.
  • Subtypes: Superficial spreading (~60–70%, long radial phase; women→legs/shin, men→back/trunk); Nodular (vertical growth from onset, aggressive — use the EFG rule: Elevated, Firm, Growing); Lentigo maligna melanoma (elderly face, chronic UV; dermoscopy: asymmetric follicular openings, rhomboids, gray peppering); Acral lentiginous (palms/soles/nails, UV-independent; commonest subtype in darker skin; dermoscopy parallel-ridge pattern = malignant vs parallel-furrow = benign).
  • Subungual melanoma: longitudinal melanonychia, irregular band, Hutchinson sign (pigment onto nail fold).
  • Diagnosis: ABCDE (Asymmetry, Border, Colour, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution), ugly-duckling sign; dermoscopy (atypical network, blue-white veil, atypical vessels); excisional biopsy ("if in doubt, cut it out"). 7-point checklist.
  • Prognosis: Breslow thickness is the key factor (+ ulceration, mitoses). Survival: <1 mm ~95–99%; 1–2 mm ~80–90%; 2–4 mm ~70–80%; >4 mm ~50–60%.
  • Risk factors: fair skin/light eyes/red hair, high & atypical nevus count, personal/family history, blistering sunburns. Dark/brown eyes are protective.

sections.title: Bacterial infections
sections.html:
InfectionOrganismKey features
Impetigo (non-bullous)β-hemolytic strep (± staph)Golden-yellow "honey" crusts; school children
Bullous impetigoS. aureus (toxin)Fragile bullae → circinate erosions
Erysipelasβ-hemolytic strepSharply-demarcated, indurated, bright-red plaque (upper dermis/lymphatics); legs/face; fever
Furuncle / carbuncleS. aureusPerifollicular abscess; carbuncle = confluent furuncles
EcthymaStrep/staphPunched-out ulcer with crust (shins)
ErythrasmaCorynebacterium minutissimumBrown scaly intertriginous patches; coral-red on Wood's light (porphyrin)
Trichomycosis axillarisCorynebacterium tenuisNodules on axillary hair shafts
Pitted keratolysisMicrococcus/KytococcusMalodorous pits on weight-bearing soles

High-yield: the "NOT caused by streptococcus" answer is the furuncle (S. aureus). Erysipelas, non-bullous impetigo, scarlet fever, cellulitis = strep.

sections.title: Viral infections
sections.html:
Herpes simplex (HSV-1/HSV-2)

  • dsDNA virus; latency in sensory ganglia → recurrences (triggers: stress, fever, UV, immunosuppression, menstruation). Grouped vesicles on erythematous base. ~70–80% seropositive for HSV-1, 25–30% for HSV-2. Asymptomatic shedding drives transmission.
  • Tzanck smear = multinucleated giant cells (cannot distinguish HSV from VZV); gold standard = viral culture/PCR. Rx: acyclovir/valacyclovir/famciclovir; suppressive therapy for frequent recurrences.

Varicella-zoster (VZV = HHV-3)

  • Chickenpox (primary) → latency in dorsal-root ganglia → herpes zoster (shingles) on reactivation; unilateral dermatomal painful vesicles (thoracic most common ~56%). Age is the main risk factor (>50 y).
  • Can cause motor involvement (segmental weakness, Ramsay-Hunt facial palsy). Post-herpetic neuralgia = commonest complication (pain >90 days; 10–15%, ↑ with age).

HPV (warts)

  • >200 types; transmitted by contact/autoinoculation; Koebnerises; black dots = thrombosed capillaries. Variants: verruca vulgaris, plantaris, plana, condylomata acuminata.
  • Genital warts = HPV 6, 11 (low-risk). Oncogenic 16, 18 → cervical/oropharyngeal SCC. Rx: salicylic acid, cryotherapy, imiquimod, podophyllotoxin (genital), laser; 9-valent vaccine.

Others

  • Molluscum contagiosum – poxvirus; dome-shaped umbilicated papules; Henderson-Patterson (molluscum) bodies; self-limiting.
  • Orf / milker's nodules – parapoxvirus from sheep/goats/cows; umbilicated bullae on hands.
  • Hand-foot-mouth – Coxsackie A16 / enterovirus 71; oral + palm/sole vesicles in children.

sections.title: Fungal infections
sections.html:
Dermatophytes (tinea)

  • Keratinophilic filamentous fungi (Trichophyton, Microsporum, Epidermophyton). Named by site: capitis (scalp — mainly children; black-dot/grey-patch, kerion, favus), corporis, cruris (inguinal), pedis (athlete's foot), manuum, unguium (onychomycosis), faciei.
  • Dx: KOH direct microscopy, culture (SDA), Wood's light (some capitis fluoresce), PCR. Rx: topical azoles/allylamines; oral griseofulvin, itraconazole, terbinafine. Emerging terbinafine-resistant T. indotineae.

Candida (yeasts)

  • C. albicans — normal flora, dimorphic (budding yeast + pseudohyphae/true hyphae). Host factors: diabetes, Cushing's, immunosuppression, steroids, anti-IL-17 therapy, AIDS (esophageal candidiasis = AIDS-defining).
  • Forms: oral thrush (pseudomembranous), erythematous/denture stomatitis, angular cheilitis, intertrigo, paronychia, onychomycosis, balanitis/vulvovaginitis, chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis (STAT1 GoF). Rx: topical nystatin/azoles; oral fluconazole/itraconazole.

Malassezia

  • Pityriasis (tinea) versicolor – hypo/hyper-pigmented scaly macules; KOH = "spaghetti and meatballs" (hyphae + yeast); not readily person-to-person.
  • Seborrheic dermatitis / dandruff and Malassezia folliculitis (no comedones).

sections.title: Sexually transmitted infections
sections.html:
Syphilis (Treponema pallidum, Gram-negative spirochete, non-culturable)

  • Primary (10–90 d): hard, painless chancre + ipsilateral (regional) lymphadenopathy.
  • Secondary (6 wk–6 mo): generalized exanthem incl. palms & soles, condylomata lata, mucous patches, systemic symptoms, generalized lymphadenopathy.
  • Latent = asymptomatic. Tertiary: gummas, cardiovascular, neurosyphilis (tabes dorsalis, general paresis, Charcot joints).
  • Congenital: Hutchinson's triad = notched incisors + interstitial keratitis + sensorineural deafness (neurosyphilis is NOT in the triad).
  • Dx: dark-field/PCR (ulcer stage); non-treponemal VDRL/RPR (monitor activity/treatment); treponemal TPHA/FTA-abs/EIA (stay positive for life). Rx = penicillin; no lasting immunity.

Gonorrhea (Neisseria gonorrhoeae)

  • Gram-negative diplococcus, intracellular in neutrophils. Urethritis/dysuria; asymptomatic in ~50% women. Dx: Gram stain, culture, NAAT. Rx: ceftriaxone IM (+azithromycin for co-infection); rising resistance.

Chlamydia (C. trachomatis)

  • Obligate intracellular; 50–70% asymptomatic; commonest bacterial STI, peak 15–24 y; PID/infertility risk. Dx: NAAT, DIF, EIA. Rx: azithromycin or doxycycline (penicillin-resistant).

Other STIs

  • Genital herpes – HSV-2 (85%); recurrent; asymptomatic shedding. HPV/condyloma (6/11). HIV – retrovirus, ↓CD4, opportunistic infections (candidiasis, Kaposi/HHV-8, hairy leukoplakia). Trichomoniasis (T. vaginalis) – frothy malodorous discharge, metronidazole. Pubic lice (Phthirus pubis). Syphilis/gonorrhea ↑ HIV transmission.

sections.title: Infestations — scabies & lice
sections.html:
Scabies (Sarcoptes scabiei)

  • Skin-to-skin (incl. sexual) transmission; intense nocturnal itch, burrows in web spaces/wrists/genitals.
  • Primary infestation: itch appears after ~4–6 weeks (sensitisation); re-infestation within 24–48 h.
  • Crusted (Norwegian) scabies: massive mite burden with thick hyperkeratotic crusts in the immunosuppressed (HIV/AIDS, elderly, steroids); extremely contagious. Rx: topical permethrin ± oral ivermectin.

Pediculosis (lice)

  • Head lice: nits cluster in the occipital & retro-auricular scalp. Pubic lice (Phthirus pubis) — genital itch, nits on pubic hair. Rx: permethrin.

sections.title: Hair & alopecia
sections.html:
Hair cycle

Anagen (growth, years; 80–90% of scalp hairs) → catagen (regression) → telogen (rest) → exogen (shedding). ~100,000 scalp follicles.

Non-scarring alopecias (follicle preserved)

  • Androgenetic alopecia (AGA) – most common; higher in white populations; progressive, symmetrical, androgen-dependent, genetically predisposed. Men → Hamilton-Norwood; women → Ludwig / "Christmas-tree". Rx: minoxidil, finasteride/dutasteride, spironolactone, PRP, transplant. (Not cyclosporine.)
  • Alopecia areata – autoimmune, non-scarring, well-defined round patches, exclamation-mark hairs, nail pitting; assoc. thyroid disease, vitiligo, atopy, Down syndrome. Types: patchy, totalis, universalis, ophiasis. Rx: steroids, JAK inhibitors, immunotherapy.
  • Telogen effluvium – diffuse shedding 2–3 months after a trigger (childbirth, fever, illness, COVID-19, stress, crash diet, thyroid); self-limiting.
  • Anagen effluvium – rapid loss (chemotherapy, radiation, thallium poisoning); reversible (stem cells spared).
  • Trichotillomania – compulsive hair-pulling (children; right temporal in right-handers); hairs of varying length, normal scalp. Psychological.
  • Traction alopecia – chronic pulling/tight styles; can become permanent.

Scarring (cicatricial) alopecias

Irreversible follicle destruction — primary (e.g. lichen planopilaris, discoid LE) or secondary (trauma, burns, radiation, infection, tumours).

sections.title: Nail disorders
sections.html:
SignAssociation
PittingPsoriasis; alopecia areata
Onycholysis (plate separates from bed)Psoriasis, fungal, trauma, thyroid
Subungual hyperkeratosis / oil-drop signPsoriasis
Koilonychia (spoon nails)Iron-deficiency anaemia
ClubbingChronic cardio-respiratory disease
Beau's lines (transverse grooves)Severe systemic illness/trauma
Pterygium, longitudinal ridgingLichen planus
Trachyonychia (rough), onychorrhexisLP, alopecia areata, ageing
Onychomadesis (shedding)Matrix insult (e.g. after HFMD)
Onychogryphosis (ram's horn)Ageing, neglect, trauma, PVD
Longitudinal melanonychia + Hutchinson signSubungual melanoma (red flag)

sections.title: Severe drug reactions & reactive dermatoses
sections.html:
Stevens–Johnson syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis (SJS/TEN)

  • Acute, mostly drug-induced (>90% of TEN), type IV cytotoxic reaction → keratinocyte apoptosis (Fas-L, granulysin). A dermatological emergency.
  • BSA split: SJS <10%, overlap 10–30%, TEN >30%. Mortality ~25–35% (TEN higher). Nikolsky positive.
  • Culprits: allopurinol, carbamazepine, lamotrigine, phenytoin, sulfonamides (cotrimoxazole), sulfasalazine, NSAIDs (oxicams). HLA links: HLA-B*1502 (carbamazepine), HLA-B*5801 (allopurinol).

Erythema nodosum

  • Tender red nodules on the shins (septal panniculitis, a hypersensitivity reaction). Causes: streptococcal infection, primary TB, sarcoidosis, IBD, drugs (sulfonamides, OCP), pregnancy, Behçet. Not rheumatoid arthritis.

sections.title: Genodermatoses & syndromic clues (high-yield)
sections.html:
SignPoints to
Ash-leaf macules, facial angiofibromas (adenoma sebaceum), shagreen patch, periungual (Koenen) fibromasTuberous sclerosis
Café-au-lait macules, neurofibromas, Crowe sign (axillary freckling), Lisch nodulesNeurofibromatosis-1
Extreme photosensitivity, early multiple skin cancers (defective DNA repair)Xeroderma pigmentosum
Dysplastic nevi + melanoma + pancreatic cancer (CDKN2A)FAMMM / dysplastic nevus syndrome
Inherited acantholytic blistering (ATP2C1)Hailey-Hailey (benign familial pemphigus)

Exam trap: in the tuberous-sclerosis MCQ the right answer is periungual fibroma; "Crowe sign" is the NF-1 distractor, "Auspitz" is psoriasis.

Hidradenitis suppurativa

Chronic follicular occlusive disease of apposed skin (axillae, groin, inframammary). Nodules → abscesses → sinus tracts → scarring. Hurley I–III staging. Rx: doxycycline (100 mg BD × 3 mo), biologics (adalimumab, secukinumab, bimekizumab), surgery.

sections.title: Dermatopathology, immunofluorescence & pharmacology summary
sections.html:
Immunofluorescence patterns

DiseaseDIF / antigen
Pemphigus vulgarisIntercellular ('chicken-wire') IgG/C3; anti-DSG3(±1); suprabasal split
Pemphigus foliaceusAnti-DSG1; subcorneal split
Bullous pemphigoidLinear IgG/C3 at BMZ; anti-BP180/BP230; epidermal side of salt-split skin
EBA / MMPDermal (floor) side of salt-split skin; anti-collagen VII / laminin-332
Dermatitis herpetiformisGranular IgA at dermal papillary tips

Antibody cheat-sheet

  • SLE: ANA, anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm. Systemic sclerosis: anti-centromere (limited), anti-Scl-70 (diffuse), anti-RNA pol III. Dermatomyositis: ANA, anti-Jo-1, anti-Mi-2. RA: RF, anti-CCP.

Antifungal mechanisms

  • Cell membrane: amphotericin B, nystatin (polyenes). Ergosterol synthesis: azoles, allylamines (terbinafine), morpholines. DNA: flucytosine. Antimitotic: griseofulvin. Glucan synthesis: echinocandins.

Biologics by target

  • Psoriasis: anti-TNF (adalimumab/etanercept/infliximab), anti-IL-12/23 (ustekinumab), anti-IL-17 (secukinumab), anti-IL-23 (guselkumab/risankizumab).
  • Atopic dermatitis: dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα); JAK inhibitors.
  • Hidradenitis suppurativa: adalimumab, secukinumab, bimekizumab.

Quick pharmacology facts

  • Stratum-corneum lipid matrix = main percutaneous absorption barrier. Tetracyclines chelate dairy/antacids/iron (don't co-administer). Permethrin = topical ectoparasiticide. Hydroxychloroquine for lupus (check G6PD/retina). Isotretinoin for severe acne (teratogen).
